content:
最近校招面试了不少 985 实习生，一个很强烈的感受是：AI 工具正在把学生之间的资源差距快速放大。

从我这段时间的感受看，差距真的可能到 5 倍。那些明显领先很多的同学，无一例外都比较早接触了 Claude Code、Codex 这类最好的 AI Coding 工具，也愿意自己花钱订阅。他们不只是会用工具，而是已经把它们变成了日常学习和开发的一部分。聊模型、工程、Agent、代码实践，可以很自然地聊一个小时，很多地方甚至比我理解得更深。

但也有不少同学，学习成绩很好，计算机基础也扎实，只是因为家庭条件或预算有限，平时只能用国内中转站或更便宜的国产模型套餐，这个差距非常现实。现在最好的国产模型和国外一线模型，在 模型综合能力上大概还有半年左右的时间差。以前没有 AI 的时候，资源差距最多只是效率差一点，现在会直接影响视野、实践深度和自信心。

这件事我最近一直在琢磨，对一些本来很有潜力的学生来说，只是因为每个月几十美元的门槛，就少接触了很多东西，我觉得挺可惜。想起我当时读大学的时候其实也经常阆中羞涩，好在大二认识了不少大牛学长带着我开始做商业项目养好了自己，挺感谢当时很耐心教我技术的各种朋友。

所以我想做一个很小的尝试：拿一部分 X 发推收益，资助几位学习成绩不错、喜欢计算机、但家庭条件不太宽裕的同学，给他们提供几个月 Claude Code 或类似 AI Coding 工具的订阅，让他们真正体验一下目前比较好的模型和开发方式。

不过我自己平时也比较忙，不太可能一个人长期处理申请、筛选、核验和跟进。所以也想顺便邀请一些对此有想法的朋友一起做。比如高校老师、学生负责人、开源社区成员，或者本身就在做教育、公益、开发者社区的朋友，可以一起帮忙想一个更靠谱的机制。

这件事我暂时不想做大，也不想做成很复杂。先从小规模实验开始，比如每期 5 到 6 个同学，每人 3 到 4 个月，甚至也可以每周来找我视频问一些问题。想着优先看推荐、作品、学习记录和真实使用反馈，尽量避免变成单纯发钱或者拼谁会写申请，当然现在能上 X 的大学生应该不缺这个钱，所以还是需要看有没有更好的渠道。

如果你有靠谱渠道，或者愿意一起参与规则设计、推荐、审核、跟进，欢迎私信我。能把这件事做得更可信一点，也许真的能帮到几个本来就很有潜力的同学。

content:
It's really impressive that BytePlus is running full GLM-5.1 at about a fifth of https://t.co/XtI4mtrDQd's direct pricing.

It's the actual model, same weights, sitting on their Coding Plan and working inside Cursor, Claude Code, TRAE and OpenClaw.

Holds 8 hour autonomous runs without falling apart, which is usually where these setups start drifting. MiniMax M2.7 is on the same plan for agent workflows that tend to break after a few tool calls. Auto routing covers GLM-5.1, DeepSeek V3.2, Kimi K2.5, GPT-OSS and a couple others, so you're not stuck picking the wrong model for the job.

Anyone still paying https://t.co/XtI4mtrDQd direct should take a look.

https://t.co/WFMr4XvOOI

How secure is the new "auto mode" for AI coding assistants? 

Researchers from HKUST and ETH Zurich put Claude Code's permission system to the test. 

They found it misses dangerous actions when a tas
content:
How secure is the new "auto mode" for AI coding assistants? 

Researchers from HKUST and ETH Zurich put Claude Code's permission system to the test. 

They found it misses dangerous actions when a task's risk is ambiguous. In a stress test, it missed 81% of risky actions, far higher than reported, especially when the AI edits files instead of using the command line.

Measuring the Permission Gate: A Stress-Test Evaluation of Claude Code's Auto Mode

Paper: https://t.co/4g4DUI1qKT
Code: https://t.co/XH1Oo0EB1i

Our report: https://t.co/JRoQjUrCVQ

The setup:

Moonshot AI (Chinese lab) shipped Kimi Code, an autonomous coding agent on kimi-k2.6

The kicker: it's a drop-in replacement
content:
A Chinese AI Model Just Hot-Swapped Claude Code in 30 Seconds

The setup:

Moonshot AI (Chinese lab) shipped Kimi Code, an autonomous coding agent on kimi-k2.6

The kicker: it's a drop-in replacement for Claude Code

Two environment variables. Same CLI. Different brain

Where the edge lives
• 100 tokens/sec - meaningfully faster than most Western incumbents
• 262,144-token context window - entire codebases in one shot
• 2 env vars + 60 seconds to migrate off Claude
• $0 extra cost - included in standard Kimi membership
• 7-day quota cycle (no rollover)

Fastest way to copy-trade anyone: https://t.co/7qw0SmTB5j

Where the Bear Case Hides
• Personal use only - no enterprise/commercial tier (yet)
• Ecosystem still tiny vs. Cursor/Copilot
• 7-day quota = retention friction
• Compatible with Claude Code cuts both ways - Anthropic can break the API surface anytime

title:
These are all on the money. Thoughts on the first two:

1) Pretty insane how quickly share shifts can happen in coding, the first real killer use case of agentic AI. I was at a Claude Code meetup last
content:
These are all on the money. Thoughts on the first two:

1) Pretty insane how quickly share shifts can happen in coding, the first real killer use case of agentic AI. I was at a Claude Code meetup last night and it blew my mind that people I'd consider tinkerers haven't even tried Codex or haven't played them off each other. Just reminds you how early we are. ChatGPT is going to make a massive run here soon I'd wager as it wins more mindshare for coding (I was already under the impression it had).

>Ultimately wonder if this means massive TAM with multiple winners (OAI, Anthropic, +others) or if all the surplus goes to consumers and doesn't accrue to anyone. No moat once models get good enough.
>Personally, I don't think no-moat happens. There will always be an advantage for those who iterate and arb early access to the best models. Use more tokens, create value with those tokens, capture or enable capture of that value (as Dylan from SemiAnalysis put it on Invest Like the Best).

2) Despite OpenAI having a competitive option in Codex, Claude has so much product enthusiasm going for it: how easy it is to work with, how much it does outside of code, how fun it is to engage with as a coworker for docs, slides, etc. + incredible shipping velocity. All of that positions Anthropic to be a broader "chief of staff" / true AI intelligence layer and win mindshare as an 'AI product company' not just the code company. Worth noting.

で、大事なのはもう１つ。炎上・アンチ・ネガティブコメントの排除です。

今月に入ってから各種SNSのネガティブコメントがやけに抑制、もしくは削除されていると。

content:
AIそのもののコストが急激に下がっていて、
それによるSNSの影響は２つあります。

１つは皆さんも恩恵を受けている自動言語翻訳。

で、大事なのはもう１つ。炎上・アンチ・ネガティブコメントの排除です。

なんとなく感じているのではないでしょうか。

今月に入ってから各種SNSのネガティブコメントがやけに抑制、もしくは削除されていると。

利用者を増やすこと、滞在時間を増やすことを目的に
また技術的に仕方なく、炎上やバトルをするようなアルゴリズムになっていました。

が、そのトレンドも変わりつつあります。

そもそもSNSは利用者を増やすフェーズから法人売上を増やすフェーズに入っています。

であればかつてのYoutubeが辿った歴史のように、炎上芸から信頼性担保する成熟したプラットフォームへ移ることでしょう。

これにAIの技術発展が組み合わさります。

利用者が増えるかどうかは本質的にネットワーク効果の価値の総量に依存します。

つまり、自動翻訳した時点で全ての言語圏がつながり合い価値は何倍にも跳ね上がります。そして利用者も伴って増えるでしょう。

滞在時間については適切なAIレコメンドがそれを可能にします。そしてネガティブ・アンチコメントの排除もまたAIが可能となります。

SNSは変革の時を迎えており、

これは船を漕ぐ人たちが正しく評価され、
タダ飯で文句だけ言っている人たちは船に乗ることさえ許されなくなる。

そういった変化が起きているということです。

やることはただ誠実に、そして真面目に働くことだけです。

title:
Researchers sent the same resume to an AI hiring tool twice. Same qualifications. Same experience. Same skills. One version was written by a real human. The other was rewritten by ChatGPT.

The AI pic
content:
Researchers sent the same resume to an AI hiring tool twice. Same qualifications. Same experience. Same skills. One version was written by a real human. The other was rewritten by ChatGPT.

The AI picked the ChatGPT version 97.6% of the time.

A team from the University of Maryland, the National University of Singapore, and Ohio State just published the receipt. They took 2,245 real human-written resumes pulled from a professional resume site from before ChatGPT existed, so the human writing was actually human. Then they had seven of the most-used AI models in the world rewrite each one. GPT-4o. GPT-4o-mini. GPT-4-turbo. LLaMA 3.3-70B. Qwen 2.5-72B. DeepSeek-V3. Mistral-7B.

Then they asked each AI to pick the better resume. Every model picked itself.

GPT-4o hit 97.6%. LLaMA-3.3-70B hit 96.3%. Qwen-2.5-72B hit 95.9%. DeepSeek-V3 hit 95.5%. The real human almost never won.

Then the researchers tried the obvious objection. Maybe the AI is just better at writing. So they had real humans grade the resumes for actual quality and ran the experiment again, controlling for it. The result was worse. Each AI kept picking itself even when human judges rated the human-written version as clearer, more coherent, and more effective.

It gets worse. The AIs do not just prefer AI over humans. They prefer themselves over other AIs. DeepSeek-V3 picked its own resumes 69% more often than LLaMA's. GPT-4o picked its own 45% more often than LLaMA's. Each model can recognize and reward its own dialect.

Then the researchers ran the simulation that ends careers. Same job. 24 occupations. Same qualifications. The only variable was whether the candidate used the same AI as the screening tool. Candidates using that AI were 23% to 60% more likely to be shortlisted. Worst gap was in sales, accounting, and finance.

99% of large companies now run AI on incoming resumes. Most of them use GPT-4o. The paper just proved GPT-4o picks GPT-4o 97.6% of the time.

If you wrote your own cover letter this week, you did not lose to a better candidate. You lost to a worse candidate who paid OpenAI 20 dollars.

Your qualifications do not matter if the AI prefers its own handwriting over yours.

title:
Stanford just proved Claude, GPT, and Gemini are all secretly running at a fraction of their real creative capacity.

And one prompt unlocks the version they hide from you.

Every time you ask an AI a
content:
Stanford just proved Claude, GPT, and Gemini are all secretly running at a fraction of their real creative capacity.

And one prompt unlocks the version they hide from you.

Every time you ask an AI a question, it calculates a vast range of possible answers.

Some are brilliant. Some are weird. Some are groundbreaking.

But it almost never gives them to you.

Because of how these models are trained through human feedback, they suffer from a massive flaw called "mode collapse."

They are hardwired to play it incredibly safe. They naturally gravitate toward the most typical, familiar, and boring response possible.

The AI knows the genius answer. It just buries it to maximize its safety score.

Researchers published a paper exposing a backdoor to bypass this filter completely.

It’s called Verbalized Sampling.

When you ask for a single answer, the AI defaults to the most predictable output.

But if you ask the AI to generate 5 different answers and assign a mathematical probability to each one, the safety loop breaks.

By forcing the model to state the probability of its own outputs, you give it permission to explore the "tails of the distribution."

It stops giving you the 99% probability generic slop. It unlocks the 5% probability brilliance.

The results are mind-bending.

In rigorous testing, this single prompting technique boosted creative output and diversity by up to 2.1x across the most powerful models on the market.

All without sacrificing factual accuracy or safety.

title:
Google DeepMind just killed the biggest assumption in computer vision.

If an AI can draw, it can see.

For years, the AI world was split in two. We had models that generated images and models that un
content:
Google DeepMind just killed the biggest assumption in computer vision.

If an AI can draw, it can see.

For years, the AI world was split in two. We had models that generated images and models that understood them.

The assumption was simple: making a picture is completely different from reading one.

That assumption is officially dead.

DeepMind just published a paper proving that image generation is the exact equivalent of LLM pretraining.

If a model learns how to generate the visual world with hyper-realistic textures and natural lighting, it secretly builds a perfect understanding of the physics, geometry, and depth of that world.

They took an image generator, Nano Banana Pro, and did something ridiculously simple.

They didn't train it to do complex math. They didn't train it to calculate depth.

They just asked it to draw its answers as pictures.

Want a depth map? Draw the closer objects brighter. 
Want surface normals? Draw left-facing surfaces in pink and right-facing in blue. 
Want semantic segmentation? Paint the target red.

The results rewrite the rules of visual AI.

This unified generative model didn't just compete with specialist models. It beat them.

It surpassed SAM 3 on segmentation. It beat Depth Anything V3 on metric depth estimation.

In zero-shot settings. With zero real-world training data. Without even knowing the camera parameters.

For anyone building autonomy stacks or diving deep into robotics, this changes the architecture entirely. You don't need a dozen separate, highly-tuned specialist models to help a machine read a room anymore.

The model just draws the correct answers because it fundamentally understands how reality looks.

↳ The difference between top-k and top-p sampling
↳ Why autoregressive models work differently from masked models

The advanced concepts that separate good from great:
↳ How RAG works and why it beats fine-tuning for factual accuracy
↳ Why Chain-of-Thought prompting makes models dramatically smarter
↳ What Mixture of Experts is and why every frontier model uses it now
↳ Zero-shot vs few-shot learning and when each one wins

The math questions that make people sweat:
↳ Why softmax is used inside attention and not something simpler
↳ What cross-entropy loss actually measures
↳ What KL divergence is and where it shows up in AI training
↳ Why vanishing gradients were destroying transformers and how they fixed it

If you are applying for any AI role in 2026 and you cannot answer at least 40 of these, you are not ready yet.

The full list of 50 questions is worth printing out and going through one by one.

Save this post. Your next interviewer has almost certainly pulled from this exact list.

title:
Chamath Palihapitiya, one of the most connected investors in tech and his warning is the clearest framing of the AI compute crisis anyone has put into words.

"It is a five alarm fire for them. They n
content:
Chamath Palihapitiya, one of the most connected investors in tech and his warning is the clearest framing of the AI compute crisis anyone has put into words.

"It is a five alarm fire for them. They need to have land, power, shell."

He's talking about Anthropic and OpenAI and the threat he's describing is called the Friendster effect.

Friendster was the dominant social network before MySpace and Facebook and it didn't lose because it had a bad product but rather it lost because it couldn't keep the site up.

Demand outpaced infrastructure, the experience degraded, and users left  for one that actually worked.

Chamath's argument is that OpenAI and Anthropic are approaching exactly that moment.

The numbers are already showing it, Anthropic is growing so fast that it had to cut Claude's thinking depth during peak hours, cap agentic sessions, and test removing Claude Code from its $20 plan entirely.

GitHub Copilot paused new signups, paying enterprise customers are hitting usage walls they've never seen before.

Dario Amodei himself admitted there is "no hedge on earth" against the risk of over-purchasing compute meaning he's deliberately staying lean on capacity, even as the demand wall approaches.

The core problem is structural, OpenAI and Anthropic grew up renting capacity from hyperscalers AWS, Azure, Google Cloud.

That was fine when they were small but now they're so large that dependency is a strategic liability.

Every token they sell runs on someone else's infrastructure and every capacity decision belongs to someone else.

And when demand spikes faster than anyone planned, there's nothing they can do in real time except throttle.

Building your own infrastructure takes 18 to 24 months minimum, you need to acquire land, secure power, construct shell and none of that happens fast.

That's why Google's $40 billion commitment to Anthropic this week is about more than just money but rather about securing the land, power, and infrastructure that Anthropic needs to not become Friendster.

Whoever controls the compute controls the frontier and right now, the AI labs with the best products are the most dependent on infrastructure they don't own.

content:
採用広報におけるAI活用は、効率化だけでなく“候補者視点の強化”と“情報発信の質向上”を同時に実現できる点で非常に有効だと感じる。
採用広報は、求人票作成、社員インタビュー、記事執筆、SNS運用、競合分析など、限られたリソースで多くの業務を回さなければならない。ここにAIを組み込むことで、企画〜執筆〜編集までの工数を大幅に削減でき、初稿作成のスピードは一気に上がる。

さらにAIは、候補者が求める情報や市場トレンドを客観的に整理できるため、企業視点ではなく“候補者が本当に知りたい情報”を発信しやすくなる。SNS運用との相性も良く、投稿案の生成や反応率の分析まで支援してくれるので、継続性と改善サイクルが強化される。

ただし、AI任せにしすぎると文章の温度感が薄くなる。採用広報で最も大事なのは、社員のリアルな声や企業文化の“熱”を伝えること。
だからこそ、AIで効率化しつつ、最後の仕上げは人が行う“ハイブリッド型”が最適解。

AIは採用広報の強力な武器になる。
使い方次第で、情報発信の質もスピードも大きく変わる。

npm install -g @ gitlawb/openclaude
